coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based composites electrocatalysts in hydrogen evolution reaction and oxygen evolution reaction: A mini review

coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen, as a perfect energy carrier, has the characteristics of high energy density and harmless combustion products, and become a clean energy to answer the energy disaster and reduce carbon emissions. Among the current methods of hydrogen production, electrocatalytic technology combined with renewable energy is an important development direction of sustainable energy supply. However, it is limited by low energy conversion efficiency and the need for expensive electrocatalysts in the actual production process. In this background, carbon-based nanocomposites become a hot research direction, as a hopeful alternative to the noble metal catalysts currently used. As a member of the family of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention owing to their advantages of large specific surface area, low in price, innocuity, high conductivity and abundant functional groups on the surface. CDs have been used in the field of electrocatalysis in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) as functional materials. The review focuses on the latest research progress in the application of CDs-based composites in HER and OER. The preparation methods of CDs and CDs-based composites, the advantages of CDs in electrocatalytic applications, and the problems faced in the advance of CDs-based composites are discussed. The review hopes to offer some visions into the recent challenges and research directions in the future for this field based on the perspective of CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen, as a perfect energy carrier, has the characteristics of high energy density and harmless combustion products, and become a clean energy to answer the energy disaster and reduce carbon emissions. Among the current methods of hydrogen production, electrocatalytic technology combined with renewable energy is an important development direction of sustainable energy supply. However, it is limited by low energy conversion efficiency and the need for expensive electrocatalysts in the actual production process. In this background, carbon-based nanocomposites become a hot research direction, as a hopeful alternative to the noble metal catalysts currently used. As a member of the family of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention owing to their advantages of large specific surface area, low in price, innocuity, high conductivity and abundant functional groups on the surface. CDs have been used in the field of electrocatalysis in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) as functional materials. The review focuses on the latest research progress in the application of CDs-based composites in HER and OER. The preparation methods of CDs and CDs-based composites, the advantages of CDs in electrocatalytic applications, and the problems faced in the advance of CDs-based composites are discussed. The review hopes to offer some visions into the recent challenges and research directions in the future for this field based on the perspective of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing energy demand around the world lead to the continuous deterioration of the energy crisis, and fossil fuels as the main energy composition is difficult to meet the energy needs of today's society [1–3]. Simultaneously, the energy crisis and environmental pollution caused by the serious consumption of fossil fuels have become the key issues to be solved in social development. Thus, the progress of clean energy conversion technology become a necessary condition for the future development of sustainable and renewable energy systems. Hydrogen, as a best energy carrier, has the characteristics of high energy density and harmless combustion products, and can be used as a clean energy to solve the energy problem and reduce carbon emissions [2,4–8]. Among them, the “green hydrogen” produced from renewable energy sources is the most suitable hydrogen energy that meets the needs of clean energy conversion. At present, the most ideal strategy to produce “green hydrogen” is to prepare hydrogen by electrolysis of water based on electrocatalytic reaction [9]. As shown in Fig. 1 , in the process of preparing hydrogen by electrolytic water, there are two half reactions, namely, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), and the working efficiency of the catalyst is the key to convert the electric energy into hydrogen energy [10]. Currently, Pt, Ir, Ru and other noble metals and their compounds have been demonstrated excellent hydrogen and oxygen evolution reaction properties [10,11]. However, noble metals have less reserves on the earth and high cost, which is not conducive to large-scale promotion and utilization [12,13]. Hence, in order to face the industrialization supplies of low cost and high efficiency, it is urgent to develop catalysts with excellent water electrolysis performance.

As a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are functional carbon-based materials, which can multi-color emission [14–16]. Since CDs were first reported, they have attracted numerous research interests owing to their excellent physical and chemical properties, especially their outstanding absorption and photoluminescence (PL) properties [17,18]. The structure of CDs is usually composed by a core of sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon, with many functional groups and polymer chains on their surface. As shown in Fig. 2 , based on the microstructure of the differences for carbon core, CDs are further refined into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [19–24]. For GQDs, their characteristics are that one or several different graphene layers constitute the carbon core with some chemical groups on their edges, and their horizontal size is usually larger than their height [24]. The CQDs are spherical structure, and have a graphite-like lattice with functional groups on the surface [24]. In contrast, CNDs are also spherical structures with different functional groups, but the difference is that the carbon core composed of amorphous carbon has no obvious lattice [19]. Notably, based on the formation process, structure and PL mechanism, the concept of CPDs was proposed in 2019 from Yang's group [19]. CPDs are aggregated or crosslinked polymer nanoparticles derived from polymers or monomers, which have distinct core-shell structure, and the core have a cross-linked polymer framework with extremely dehydrated and a small amount of graphitized carbon, and the higher performance of CPDs is attributed to the rich functional groups or polymer chains in the shell. In the past years, researchers have focused on continuously developing new preparation methods, optimizing their PL properties and mechanisms, and exploring their applications related to optical properties, so that CDs-based materials have become a functional nanomaterial that cannot be ignored in the material science and engineering application fields [25].

Currently, various carbon-based materials, including MXene, metal organic frameworks, graphene-based materials, hollow porous carbon spheres, graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, and carbon cloth, have been used as electrocatalysts for HER and OER processes [26–29]. However, the process of preparing these carbon-based materials is complex, and reducing metal precursors typically requires very strong reducing reagents [30,31]. Additionally, when metal species are loaded onto the surface of carbon-based materials, their affinity for substrates may be affected, and the large particle size and uneven distribution of metal species on the carrier can lead to a decrease in catalytic performance [31]. Moreover, the large-scale preparation of these traditional carbon-based materials is still expensive [30]. Therefore, it is desirable to find an efficient and simple way to create uniformly distributed metal nanoparticles firmly enclosed in a nanocarbon composite using a cost-effective method. CDs are an important component of carbon-based materials, and they exhibit remarkable luminescent properties. They are also low in toxicity, photostable, biocompatible, and capable of multiphoton excitation, upconverted photoluminescence, and chemiluminescence [32]. Furthermore, they have similar physicochemical properties to other carbon-based materials [33]. In comparison to traditional carbon-based catalysts, the preparation of CDs is simple and cost-effective, and CDs have abundant active sites [34]. More importantly, the small size of CDs allows metal precursors to be adsorbed onto them, preventing severe aggregation [35]. This makes it possible to manipulate the electronic properties of single atoms or small metal clusters in order to engineer multi-component catalysts with high active-site densities, which could significantly enhance catalyst activity [36]. In addition, with the inherent structural defects of CDs and further heteroatom doping, more electrocatalytic active sites can be generated, and the high specific surface area of CDs is conducive to interfacial reactions and further improving the catalytic performance of the composite material [37,38]. As a result, CDs have been extensively studied for their potential use as multifunctional precursors in the preparation of electrocatalysts [39]. The researchers based on the electrochemical of CDs have developed many efficient and stable electrocatalysts, which involve more electrocatalytic reactions including HER and OER. In summary, the electrochemical advantages of CDs are shown in the following aspects: (1) large surface area increases conductivity; (2) the rich surface states, including –OH, –NH3, and –COOH, which endow CDs with excellent modification ability and enhanced stability, and help to regulate their chemical and physical properties; (3) heteroatom doping enhances the electronic properties and electrocatalytic sites of CDs by helping intramolecular charge transfer and increasing activity; (4) low cost and low toxicity, facilitate mass production and utilization.

Based on the above difficulties in electrocatalysis for the current methods of hydrogen production, as well as the structural and performance advantages of CDs for electrocatalytic applications. In this review, recent advances in CDs-based catalysts of water electrolysis are described in detail. The preparation methods of CDs and CDs-based composites, as well as the advantages of CDs in electrochemistry and their applications in HER and OER are discussed. Finally, the challenges and opportunities of CDs-based catalysts are reviewed. This review aims to help people well comprehend the application of CDs in the field of electrocatalysis, and offer guidance for further preparation of high-performance CDs-based electrocatalysts.
In this section, the common precursors and preparation methods for obtaining CDs mainly is discusses (Fig. 3 ). Furthermore, several strategies for preparing CDs-based composites are introduced.
The carbon sources used for preparing CDs are extensive, and are described as biomass, small organic molecules, and other precursors in this review (Table 1 ).

Biomass is a friendly natural product with the advantages of low cost, easy access, green, non-toxic and renewable, which is a degradable bio-organic material that can come from grass, organic waste, food and so on [41,42]. In addition, due to the presence of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and glutathione in biomass, CDs derived from biomass are rich in heteroatoms, such as N, O, P or S, which contribute to the excellent PL performance of CDs [43,44]. Meanwhile, the conversion of biomass into CDs can not only achieve reasonable waste utilization, but also conform to Green Chemistry. Compared with traditional carbon sources, CDs of different properties and structures can be obtained due to the diversity of biomass sources. More notably, owing to the renewability of raw materials, CDs can be prepared on a large scale at a very low cost, which is of great significance for the mass production and commercial application of CDs products. Recently, in the field of electrocatalysis, people have tried to produce CDs and CDs-based composites from biomass. Liu et al. using biomass garlic as carbon source and nitrogen source, obtained a new type of self-crosslinked membrane CDs-based carrier through hydrothermal method, and then compounded CDs with Ru dots. By regulating the content of Ru dots and CDs, a composite with catalytic activity was obtained [45]. The leaves are composed of protein, plant fiber, sugar, vitamins, etc., and are rich in C, N, O and other elements. Fig. 4 A show that Li et al. prepared highly crystalline CQDs by simple treatment using ginkgo leaves [46]. It was found that N doping into the carbon matrix of CQDs could regulate the electronic properties of adjacent carbon atoms by intramolecular charge transfer, and help to promote the electrochemical properties. Therefore, the advantages of using biomass such as leaves are simple operation, easy implementation, and low cost. Meanwhile, biomass has also become a green natural carbon source for large-scale preparation of CQDs.

Many organic molecules have been used as carbon source to prepare CDs. Citric acid, a weak organic acid, as the kind of carbon sources, contains many –COOH and –OH groups on the structure, which allow the inheritance of multiple functional groups on the obtained CDs. Furthermore, citric acid can be combined with other precursors, thereby increasing the effective conjugate length in the CDs, which makes it a commonly used precursor choice for preparing CDs (Fig. 4B). Citric acid-based CDs show both PL from blue to red regions and extremely high quantum yield [50]. Therefore, the development of citric acid-based CDs is extensive. Li et al. selected citric acid and poly(ethylenimine) as precursors to prepared CDs with an average size of about 4.5 nm based on heating the aqueous solution of precursors at 110 °C for 2 h [51]. CDs were obtained using citric acid as a precursor and further combined with Ru to prepare CDs-based composites [52]. It was found that the binding of CDs to Ru1CoP could effectively prevent particle aggregation to increase the exposure of active reactive centers, thus improving the catalytic properties of CDs-based composites, which opens a new approach for the development of carbon-based nanomaterials with electrocatalytic properties.

The aniline-like compounds have become an important class of CDs preparation precursors. They have self-doped N elements, which can be easily oxidized and polymerized into large conjugated structures, thereby providing CDs with different structures and properties. Therefore, aniline-like compounds play an important role in the preparation of CDs with advanced physical and chemical properties. Phenylenediamine (PD), as an aromatic compound, has two amino groups with high activity in the structure, enabling PD to produce a variety of oxidation products and further crosslinking, carbonization or polymerization under appropriate conditions to product CDs. Recent research advances on the PD-derived CDs have been discussed in our previous work [53]. Meanwhile, we also used PD as a carbon source to obtain various performance CDs [48,54–57]. For example, the crude product of CDs was obtained with o-PD and hydroquinone as carbon source through the oxidation/polymerization and Schiff base reaction at room temperature without the addition of oxidant [48]. As shown in Fig. 4C, based on the polar differences of the crude product components, two kinds of CDs (YCDs and GCDs) were obtained after separation by silicone chromatography columns. Different surface states conferred different properties to two kinds CDs. The surface of YCDs was rich in –NO2 and –OH groups to give them a narrow band gap, resulting in a redshift of their PL emission peak to 555 nm. Based on the inner filter effect, the YCDs were used as probes for the quantitative analysis of the toxic pollutant p-nitrophenol. The surface of GCDs with rich –NH2 groups could interact with O–H/O-D. Due to O–H, the quenching effect of oscillations on GCDs was higher than that of O-D oscillation, so GCDs could be used for the detection of H2O content in D2O. In Fig. 4D, Shi et al. prepared Sm3+-doped CDs (Sm/CDs) using a hydrothermal method with citric acid, urea, and SmCl3 as precursors. The Sm/CDs demonstrate efficient generation of reactive oxygen species and photothermal conversion capabilities for fluorescence imaging-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy under NIR light excitation, making them promising photosensitizers for PDT.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, has strong reducibility and can be used as an electron donor to reduce other molecules in the human body. The properties of AA endow it with the ability to interact with harmful free radicals, thereby protecting cells and tissues from oxidative damage. Hence, AA has been often selected as carbon sources in the preparation of CDs [58]. These CDs usually exhibited outstanding good stability and could interact with a variety of ions. Therefore, it could be used for quantitative analysis of these targets. For example, Zeng et al. developed a new type of sensor based on CDs to detect kaempferol, and CDs were prepared by low temperature oil bath reaction with ascorbic acid as carbon source, which had excellent PL and up-conversion PL properties [59]. CDs were prepared by microwave method using ascorbic acid and β -alanine as precursor, and were applied to bioimaging of cells [60]. Besides the small molecules as described above, some other researchers also reported the preparation of CDs from small organic molecules, such as dye, glucose and tannic acid [50].

Microorganisms, including bacteria, yeasts and algae, have been utilized as carbon sources for the preparation of CDs [61]. Nu et al. reported a green technique to prepare CDs from microalgae with a microwave-assisted treatment [62]. Larger carbon sources, such as carbon nanotubes, carbon fibres, and graphite rods were selected as precursors to obtain CDs [25]. For example, Qiao et al. studied a simplistic method to produce CQDs with excitation-wavelength dependent PL [63]. Three typical activated carbons were used as carbon sources, including coal activated carbon, wood activated carbon and coconut activated carbon. CDs usually require long-wavelength emission for some applications. Therefore, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) have become a hopeful precursor for the preparation of CDs [64]. PAHs have a large conjugated structure, which promotes the formation of long-wavelength CDs with large sp2 domains. Then, as a nanoprobe for the development of new CDs with tunable optical properties, they have received extensive attention. Long et al. reported that G-CDs with high PL quantum yield were prepared based on a microwave assisted solvothermal method using amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, and ethanol as solvent [64].

Based on the size difference of the precursor, the preparation of CDs can be generally divided into two types of methods, including the top-down methods and the down-top methods [78,79]. The top-down methods are through the cracking and stripping of macroscopic carbon sources. The down-top methods contain the pyrolysis or carbonization process based on small organic molecules.

For the preparation of CDs in the top-down methods, the precursors used include graphene, graphene nanosheets, carbon nanotubes and activated carbon [78]. These methods depend on the mechanical or chemical fragmentation of carbon materials into smaller carbon nanoparticles, which may lead to particles of different sizes and shapes, and further processing to obtain CDs. Presently, many techniques, including electrochemical oxidation, ball milling, ultrasonic treatment and laser ablation, have been used in top-down methods to prepare CDs [80]. The top-down methods have been effectively used to the synthesis of CDs with unique electronic performances for many applications, such as energy storage and electrocatalysis. Although the top-down methods have the advantage of obtaining CDs with controllable size and morphology, they also involve harsh experimental conditions and high energy consumption, which may not be conducive to the large-scale utilization and industrial production of CDs. Furthermore, the CDs prepared by the top-down strategy contain rich graphene structures with fewer surface groups, and π-π stacking may occur between the prepared CDs, resulting in a decrease in PL intensity [78]. Overall, the top-down methods are a promising strategy to produce CDs with specific properties, but in future research, the process needs to be optimized to obtain more efficient and sustainable methods.

The down-top methods of CDs are to prepare nanoscale carbon particles using small molecular as carbon sources, which usually contains multiple steps chemical reactions, including polymerization, dehydration and carbonization, and finally forms CDs with clear size, shape and properties [47,81–83]. First, chain compound intermediates are obtained based on a variety of chemical reactions between small organic molecules. Subsequently, the intermediates polymerize and aggregate into polymer-like CDs intermediates with some carbon clusters through covalent, non-covalent or other interactions, and the obtained intermediates are carbonized to transform carbon cores based on appropriate conditions, especially at high temperatures and high pressure. Lastly, the excessive precursor can be used as a surface passivator to modify on the surface of CDs, and improve their PL properties. In addition, down-top methods can also introduce several functional groups into the CDs structure based on the difference of carbon sources, and further improve the performance of CDs in applications. However, the purification process of CDs may be a tedious and time-consuming process, and the preparation of CDs with specific properties often requires a complex regulatory process. Despite the above challenges, CDs have shown great potential in the field of electrochemistry and have been used for overall water splitting.

Due to the good electrochemical properties of CDs, it has been widely used in electrocatalytic technology. In fact, CDs need to be hybridized with many inorganic compounds, including metal nitrides, metal oxides, metal sulfides, metal phosphides, and metal alloys to obtain efficient electrocatalysts, and further applied in electrocatalysis [84]. So far, many preparation methods have been developed to obtain CDs-based composites. In general, the methods of constructing these composites are to obtain CDs with a specific structure first, and then further obtain composites by pyrolysis, hydrothermal/solvothermal, physical treatment preparation [84,85].

Pyrolysis is one of the most used methods for preparing CDs-based composites. In this method, CDs and precursors are directly heated in an inert gas atmosphere, and the reaction conditions in the reaction process are optimized on the basis of adjusting the temperature and time to obtain CDs-based composites. For example, Song et al. designed composite materials using CoP nanoparticles doped with Ru atom supported on CDs nanosheets through a facile pyrolysis method, which showed a good stability and activity for HER in alkaline and acidic medium [52]. Song et al. prepared amorphous cobalt phosphide modified F-doped CDs (F-CDs/CoP) using the method of pyrolysis [86]. The obtained F-CDs/CoP composites had superior electrocatalytic properties, and showed outstanding HER and OER activity with continual stability in KOH solution [86].

The hydrothermal/solvothermal method is to prepare CDs-based composites with different structures and morphologies by adjusting the temperature, time and solvent of the system at a specific temperature and pressure in a closed system containing a mixture of precursors, CDs and reaction solvents. Jana et al. prepared carbon-supported bimetallic nanocomposites through a hydrothermal method using the obtain CDs, Ni compounds and Co compounds as precursors for the electrocatalytic HER [87]. It was found that the catalytic performance of the composite material was attributed to its inherent layered structure, interconnected nano-array structure and redox properties, and the surface defects of CDs offered an excellent active region for the catalytic and electronic environment. Wu et al. reported that the FeCoSγ/NCDs composite was prepared by using a solvothermal method, and the obtained composites with outstanding electrocatalytic property offered an efficient method to increase metal sulfide electrocatalysts [88]. Tian et al. constructed Ni5Mo3P@CDs3 composites through a hydrothermal method, and CDs could effectively adjust the morphology, reduce the aggregation, and maintain the long-term stability to ensure their excellent HER performance [89].

CDs-based composites can be obtained by physical compound. Compared with the above method, the operation of physical compound is simple, and the as-prepared CDs and precursors can be directly mixed mechanically in water or other solvents, and then further assisted by stirring, ultrasound and other methods to obtain CDs-based composites. For example, Mubharak et al. reported the fabrication of the CQDs decorated Cu2O composite through a simply ultrasonication-assisted method [90]. Song et al. obtained B-doping CDs modified on the surface of C3N4 by the surface deposition [91].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The advantages of CDs in electrochemical
ce:section.ce:para: Noble metals, including Pt, Ir, Ru, are famous for their outstanding catalytic activity. However, large-scale preparation and application are limited due to the disadvantages of high cost and low reserves. Therefore, it is essential to develop suitable alternatives with comparable or superior activity. CDs are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, which have several advantages, including simple and cost-effective preparation methods [53]. The core of CDs has excellent conductivity, making them promising for water splitting. Furthermore, abundant surface/edge functional groups serve as active centers or provide bonding sites for creating composites/hybrids with other materials, thus improving the charge transfer rate and stability of the composites [92]. Additionally, CDs can be used as structure regulators to control the morphology of composites [93]. Among the CDs-based electrocatalysts, various controllable strategies have been effectively employed to adjust their internal electronic structure and physical-chemical properties, ultimately boosting their electrocatalytic activity. CDs play different roles, mainly categorized as enhancing electron transmission, spatial confinement, establishing CDs-metal interfaces, and heteroatom doping [94,95]. Hence, CDs have attracted much attention in the field of electrocatalysis.

In fact, the diversity of CDs provides the necessary conditions for the large-scale development of electrocatalysts [96]. First, the preparation of carbon sources is diverse, and the prepared CDs can retain the functional groups on the carbon source, which gives an innate advantage to the diversity of the CDs. Secondly, the CDs surface has many functional groups, which provide the possibility of functionalization. Finally, heteroatom doping can effectively change the chemical structure and physical properties of CDs and offer the active site, thus improving the electrocatalytic performance. Hence, the diversity of CDs brings great room for expansion, which is necessary for commercial applications.

During electrocatalysis, the electrical conductivity of the catalyst largely determines the electrocatalytic performance. The good electrical conductivity of the catalyst can help to well reduce both the Schottky barrier of the catalyst-electrolyte interface and the catalyst-electrode interface in the electrocatalytic reaction, promoting the high efficiency of energy conversion [97]. Hence, it is an important way to improve the conductivity of the nano-catalyst for an efficient electrocatalytic reaction. CDs are a new type of carbon-based materials. They not only have small size in electrochemical reactions, but also have high conductivity with fast electron transfer, and have broad application prospects in electrocatalytic reactions [39,98–100]. For example, Song et al. also further demonstrated that the carrier constructed based on CDs could improve the conductivity of the electrocatalyst during catalysis [52]. Du et al. reported that one-dimensional copper-based nanowires (GQDs@Ru, Mo–CuO/CF) were obtained through a series of reactions [1]. It was found that Mo and Ru were conducive to the dissociation of water and optimize the adsorption/desorption process of hydrogen [1]. While GQDs had edge effects and good conductivity, favor electron transport and exposure of the active site [1].

CDs can exhibit good stability in aqueous solution owing to the electrostatic stability, which can greatly improve the stability of the catalyst. Furthermore, based on the chemical stability of CDs in various media, it has also been proved to be beneficial to enhance the stability of hybrid catalysts. For CDs-based composites, CDs can serve as excellent carriers to avoid the aggregation of metals and their derivatives, thus significantly improving the catalytic activity. For example, Tian et al. demonstrated that CDs could efficiently effectively regulated the electrocatalyst morphology, reduced aggregation and promoted long-term stability, thereby enhancing their electrocatalytic performance [89]. Therefore, CDs are essential for promoting the stability of electrocatalytic catalytic performance.

CDs have been extensive studied in the field of electrocatalytic owing to their unique electrical properties. In HER, OER, and overall water splitting, the unique surface chemical properties and rich active sites of CDs promote the formation of H2 and O2, improving the kinetics. In general, the multiple advantages of CDs enable them to play important roles in the field of sustainable energy conversion, and provide great help in solving environmental pollution, ensuring clean energy production and environmentally sustainable development. However, further studies are needed to fully understand the mechanism and to optimize the process of CDs preparation.

Electrolysis of water may produce hydrogen fuel in a clean and sustainable way. The electrocatalytic cracking of water utilizes HER and OER, and the design of electrocatalysts for both has received extensive attention. HER contains a two-step two-electron reduction reaction, which can be considered as three possible reactions under different pH conditions. The first is the Volmer reaction, the electrochemical adsorption/reduction process. Specifically, a proton adsorbed on the surface of the catalytic material is coupled with the electrons to produce an adsorbed hydrogen intermediate (H*), in which * is an unoccupied active site. For different electrolytes, protons exist in different forms. H+ is a proton source in acidic medium, while the cleavage of H–O–H in alkaline medium is a proton source. Next, the adsorbed H* forms H2 through the Heyrovsky or Tafel reaction, and desorbs in turn. The Tafel reaction is a direct combination of two H * to produce H2, and the Heyrovsky reaction is that the obtained H* binds to the hydrated proton and then gets an electron from the surface of catalyst to form H2. In alkaline medium, the catalyst needs to break the H–O–H bond before adsorbing H* to form adsorbed H* and OH−, which is more difficult to accomplish than the reduction of H+. Due to the reaction kinetic barrier, it is needed to input the water decomposition reaction with external energy to overcome the barrier during the electrochemical reaction. Among them, the experimental potential between the driving electrolysis of water and the thermodynamic theoretical potential is called the overpotential. The overpotential (η) required to achieve a current density (j) of -10 mA cm−2 (η10) is usually an important measure of HER performance.

In fact, there are many catalysts that have been used for HER. Based on the highest catalytic efficiency, Pt is the benchmark for many electrocatalytic water splitting processes. Due to the high price of raw materials, Pt-based catalysts are not ideal for large-scale applications. In order to reduce costs and maintain high catalytic efficiency, Pt/CDs based composites have been widely reported. As shown in Fig. 5 A, Xiao et al. reported that a simple method for preparation of Pt, CQDs-loaded graphene composite for HER was established by electrolysis-solvothermal process [101]. Chen et al. obtained a low-Pt catalysts using defective carbon support assembled from GQDs [102]. It was found that obtained catalysts clearly improved the HER activity [102]. Besides, some non-noble metals were proposed, including Ni, Co, Mo and W based composites with CDs as catalysts for HER. For example, Yang et al. developed a Ni nanoparticle (NPs)/CQDs (Ni/CQD) composite, and estimated as an electrocatalyst for HER under the alkaline medium [103]. Based on the interface of Ni–O–C between Ni NPs and CQDs, the Ni/CQD composites displayed excellent electrocatalytic performance for HER. The onset potential was comparable to that of Pt wire, and the Tafel slope was low, which was 98 mV dec−1. Wang et al. reported a N, S co-doped CDs (NSCDs)/CoS hybrid using heat-treatment, NSCDs/CoS composites as catalyst displayed the ideal electrocatalytic activity for HER process [104]. It was studied that the outstanding electrocatalytic activity of composites for HER was due to the synergistic effect of NSCDs and CoS. Under acidic conditions, NSCDs could prevent CoS from undergoing dissolution or agglomeration, increasing the surface area of composites and the fast charge transfer efficiency. Guo et al. obtained MoS2 with a large amount of exposed edge sites for HER, and based on the GQD decoration, the electrical conductivity of MoS2 could be further improved to promote the electrocatalytic activity [105]. As shown in Fig. 5B, Song et al. reported the design and preparation of MoP/CDs nanoparticles using CDs with different N contents, and the optimized composite material exhibited excellent performance in the HER, with an overpotential of 70 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 using a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte [39]. Abraham et al. reported that WO3⋅0.33H2O/casein-derived CQDs hybrid nanostructures were prepared using a hydrothermal method, and their catalytic activity for HER was examined [98]. The electrochemical studies demonstrated that the WO3⋅0.33H2O/CQDs hybrid nanostructure exhibited efficient HER activity, with a low overpotential of 197 mV [98]. This improved performance was attributed to the synergistic effect of the hydrated tungsten oxide and CQDs, which enhanced conductivity, increased the number of accessible active sites, and facilitated better mass transport, ultimately leading to better HER activity [98]. In addition, during HER, Ru showed good electrocatalytic activity. However, the major problems of Ru-based catalysts are their limited reserves, high price, and low stability. Hence, to solve the above problems, the Ru was combined with a carbon carrier to build electrocatalysts, which helped to increase the activity of the catalyst and reduce the consumption of Ru. Li et al. explored the synergy between Ru and carbon-based carriers, and elaborated the effects of carbon-based carriers and metal crystallinity on the catalytic properties of HER. Studies have shown that the catalysts prepared by loading Ru nanoparticles on CQDs (Ru@CQDs) carriers perform better than on other carbon-based carriers [30]. In addition, the HER performance of the catalysts constructed based on Ru@CQDs was highly dependent on crystallinity and confinement. Among them, Ru nanoparticles were successfully anchored to CQDs through strong coordination interaction, which favored the confinement of Ru nanoparticles around CQDs to prevent their aggregation. Liu et al. reported that Ru dots were loaded into self-crosslinked CDs (Ru@SC-CDs) by hydrothermal process to design HER catalysts, and Ru@SC-CDs displayed outstanding HER activity with a low overpotential of 10 mA cm−2 [45]. Furthermore, CDs with several metal composites have been also used in the HER process. For example, Wang et al. prepared Ni foam‐supported NiMoO4 nanosheet arrays modified with Fe-doped CQDs (Fe‐CQDs/NiMoO4/NF), which showed an overpotential of only 117 mV for HER at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 [106]. As shown in Fig. 5C, Nguyen et al. developed a three-dimensional Pt Mo nanosponge (3D PtxMo1-x@graphene) coated with graphene dots for catalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [107]. In the low-temperature aqueous solution, the exfoliation of CDs during the co-reduction of Pt/Mo precursor and CDs caused the wrapping of graphene dots. Studies confirmed that the synergistic effect of the internal metal alloy network and the external graphene dots in 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene caused their excellent electrocatalytic properties and durability in both acidic and alkaline medium [107]. Recently, the W doping method was used to obtain Ru-based catalyst with other metals. In Fig. 5D, Jiang et al. developed a novel composite by loading W doped RuP2 nanoparticles anchored on the CDs (W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0) for HER process of wide-pH [108]. The dispersion improvement of W doping RuP2 nanoparticles was improved by a strong coordination between the metal ions and the surface functional groups of CDs. Meanwhile, the self-crosslinking structure of CDs could facilitate the electron transfer, and the W component optimized the electron configuration, thus driving the proton shift and hydrogen diffusion. Constructively, the prepared W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 could reach lower overpotential of 19 mV and 28 mV under acidic and alkaline conditions and showed good stability. Liu et al. prepared a hybrid structure of N-doped sp2/sp3 carbon interfaces loaded with Ru nanoparticles (Ru/NCDs) [109]. Studies have shown that the electronic structure of active center Ru was effectively regulated based on the interaction of Ru and NCDs, while the energy barrier of HER intermediates was reduced by the formed N-doped sp2/sp3 carbon interface, and the hydrogen adsorption and desorption were balanced, thus significantly improving the activity of Ru/NCDs. In addition, metal-free CDs-based electrocatalysts have also been reported. Sim et al. obtained nitrogen, and fluorine co-doped graphene quantum dot (N, F-GQDs) using two-step functionalizing method, and N, F-GQDs showed electrocatalytic effect with good stability [110]. Besides the above, detailed parameters of the CDs-base composites recently used for HER are shown in Table 2 , including the electrolyte environment used for the catalyst, the overpotential at a specific current density. CDs are used in combination with various metal or non-metal species to obtain electrocatalysts for the HER. In these electrocatalysts, CDs mainly exist as catalyst carriers, and the catalytic activity of CDs in HER needs more research to support.

Like HER at the cathodic, OER is also another key process of the entire water splitting device at the anodic, and many relevant research works have been reported in the literature [118–122]. OER contains four electron-four proton coupling reactions that required a high energy input to overcome kinetic obstacles. It is a complex and slow kinetic process with low efficiency, which greatly hinders the application of electrochemical water splitting in H2 production. The reaction mechanism of OER is as follows:

Ru and Ir derivatives are the most used catalysts for the OER process. However, these catalysts have the disadvantage of limited abundance and high cost. Although transition metal-based catalysts have been developed as a better alternative catalyst, they still have the disadvantages of poor stability and low efficiency. Recently, there has been a growing interest in utilizing the outstanding electrochemical properties of CDs for various applications. Researchers have developed a range of CDs-based composites specifically aimed at improving the efficiency of OER process [123–125]. These composites often incorporate one or more metals, such as Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu. As shown in Fig. 6 A, Tian et al. reported that a simple method was developed to obtain a new type of hybrid catalyst, CDs-MnO2. The resulting CDs-MnO2 catalysts have high surface areas, improved conductivity, and fast charge transfer pathways [126]. They showed significantly improved electrocatalytic activity and high stability for OER. The optimal CDs0.15-MnO2 catalyst requires only 343 mV overpotential to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2, with a relatively low Tafel slope of 43.6 mV dec−1 [126]. In Fig. 6B, Rinawati et al. immobilized Fe2+ into the GQDs to form a heterogeneous catalyst (GQDs-Fe) and activated their catalytic activity to serve as molecularly well-defined single Fe sites for OER in an alkaline medium. This process achieved an ultra-low overpotential of 223 mV (at a current density of 50 mA cm−2), outperforming the benchmark catalyst IrO2, which only achieved 357 mV [127]. Zhu et al. studied that a facile two-step method to prepare the CoP/CDs composite, which displayed optimal electrocatalytic activity and high stability for the OER process [128]. The results showed that the good electrocatalytic activity of the composite material is due to the small size of CoP and CDs, and the fast electron transfer induced by the structure of CDs [128]. In Fig. 6C, Rehman et al. reported that they prepared a composite of CDs with Ni-based mixed linkers MOF (CDs@MOF) using a solvothermal reaction method, and CDs@MOF composite showed a lower onset potential and overpotential for OER compared to other MOF-based electrocatalysts. The composite demonstrated excellent stability for about 40 h and up to 1000 CV cycles, which is comparable to state-of-the-art electrocatalysts such as RuO2 and IrO2 [129]. For multi-metal CDs-based electrocatalysts, Tang et al. used CQDs to construct composites with Ni–Fe layered double-hydroxide (NiFe-LDH) nanoplates, and the obtained CQD/NiFe-LDH showed high electrocatalytic activity and stability for OER [130]. Yang et al. prepared a complex of NiFeOx cluster with nitrogen-doped carbon (NiFeOx©NC) using one-step electrodeposition in aqueous solutions containing Ni2+, Fe3+, and CQDs, and the strongly coupled interface between NiFeOx clusters and N-doped carbon caused the enhanced OER performance superior to the commercial dimensionally stable anode [131]. Yang et al. based on the sulfur treatment of Ni foam substrate and the synergistic regulation of CDs on the morphology and properties of MnO2, obtained Ni3S2/MnO2@N, F-CQDs catalyst with outstanding OER electrocatalytic property and stability under alkaline conditions [132]. As outlined in Table 3 , many approaches showed promising results in enhancing the performance of OER processes.

Water splitting contains two half reactions, and usually two effective electrocatalysts are needed to drive the electrochemical reaction. In recent years, many nanomaterials have become abundant resources to prepare bifunctional catalysts for HER and OER [139–142]. Ideally, the bifunctional catalysts of HER and OER must operate in the same pH range for water splitting. To minimize the overpotential of the electrolysis system, water splitting is mainly carried out in strongly acidic or alkaline solutions. However, this condition poses a challenge to most non-noble metal water splitting catalysts, as highly active catalysts under acidic conditions may be inactive or unstable in alkaline electrolytes. Therefore, the simultaneous development of effective and stable bifunctional electrocatalysts to promote two half reactions must have the advantage of improving the reaction rate and efficacy. Additionally, using a single bifunctional HER-OER catalyst simplifies the design of the entire system and reduces costs. In summary, combining the advantages of HER and OER electrocatalysts, the construction of efficient HER-OER bifunctional catalyst is of great benefit to improve the overall electrolytic water activity, and in fact, the development of bifunctional electrocatalysts based CDs for HER and OER have been attempted. Table 4 presents a comprehensive summary of overall water splitting for different CDs-based electrocatalysts. Song et al. prepared novel amorphous cobalt phosphide modified F-doped CDs(F-CDs/CoP), which displayed superior electrocatalytic performance with outstanding HER and OER activity, and sustained stability [86]. For overall water splitting, F-CDs/CoP offered current density of 10 mA cm−2 and involved a low cell voltage of 1.48 V in alkaline conditions. Liu et al. prepared N-doped CDs/CoP composite with unique structure on Ni foam (CoP-NCDs/NF), and the strong interaction of CDs with CoP promoted the bifunctional catalytic activity of CoP-NCDs/NF electrode for the HER and OER, with overpotentials as low as 103 and 226 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. Furthermore, CoP-NCDs/NF exhibited outstanding overall water splitting performance, a low cell voltage of 1.55 V at 10 mA cm−2, with excellent stability in a two-electrode alkaline electrolyzer. Irshad et al. reported that they fabricated CDs decorated with silver-doped vanadium oxide (Ag/V2O5@C) using an ultrasonication approach [143]. At 10 mA cm−2, Ag/V2O5@C showed lower overpotentials of 126 mV and 388 mV for HER and OER, respectively [143]. The lower Tafel slope of 81 mV dec−1 and 71 mV dec−1 was attributed to Ag/V2O5@C for HER and OER, respectively [143]. Hedau et al. developed a bifunctional CQD@MOF electrocatalyst for water splitting [144]. The CQDs were derived from kiwi as a natural source, and the MOF was synthesized using water as a green solvent. HER and OER occurred with a current density of 50 mA cm−2 at lower overpotentials of 48 and 135 mV in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte, respectively. In a two-electrode system, the CQD@MOF/NF electrode showed a low overpotential of 1.36 V to achieve 10 mA cm−2, demonstrating the practical feasibility of overall water splitting. Additionally, the electrode exhibited high chemical stability and robustness even after 4000 cycles and more than 17 h. These results were attributed to the effective combination of substantial adsorption sites and the improved conductivity of the MOF induced by the electron-accepting characteristics of the CQDs, which have high charge transfer rates during water splitting.

CDs have broad application prospects in the field of electrocatalytic applications owing to their unique structural, electrical properties and excellent stability. Among them, CDs can fully exert the interface modification and supporting characteristics to regulate the composition, electronic structure, morphology and catalytic performance of transition metal-based materials. However, there are still many problems and challenges for the application of CDs in water electrolysis.
(1)
The trial-and-error method based on artificial experience is still the main strategy for the preparation of new nanomaterials. However, the experimental process is inevitably affected by subjectivity, randomness and contingency, which makes it difficult for people to obtain target materials with ideal properties efficiently through established methods. With the rapid development of data-driven technology, machine learning, as an important branch of artificial intelligence, has received more and more attention. However, it is still in the preliminary stage in the research and application of catalysts. Combining machine learning with the preparation of CDs-based catalysts, the optimal method of preparation is obtained through model screening, which plays an important guiding role in the design and development of catalysts.
(2)
The analysis method used for the electronic structures of the catalyst are relatively simple. In fact, the electronic structures of the catalyst have a great influence on their catalytic properties, promoting electron transfer and facilitating the adsorption and desorption of intermediates. However, the development of catalysts is relatively behind compared to the various development strategies of catalysts. Therefore, a series of advanced characterization techniques are needed to gain insight into the effects of CDs on changes in electronic structure.
(3)
In order to maximize the catalytic performance, it is necessary to increase metal loading of CDs-based composites. Therefore, in the preparation process of composite materials, it is the focus of future research to obtain CDs-based composite materials with high metal loading by using structural characteristics and optimizing the preparation process. Meanwhile, it is of great value to explore the origin of electrocatalytic activity enhancement from the perspective of atoms and molecules, which is an important theoretical basis for the preparation of high-performance electrocatalysts.

In summary, CDs-based composites have unique advantages and great potential as catalysts for HER and OER. In the future, CDs-based catalysts need to further design and develop advanced preparation strategies and characterization methods. Meanwhile, it is necessary to deeply understand the structure-performance relationship of CDs-based composites, and form an effective cycle between theory and experiment with the help of advanced computer technology.
